name: Pint (Physical Units for Python)

performance.notes: Minimal overhead; NumPy array support via pint-pandas; unit parsing cached

agent_readiness.notes: Unit-aware computation library. from pint import UnitRegistry. ureg = UnitRegistry(). distance = 5 * ureg.meter. time = 2 * ureg.second. speed = distance / time. speed.to('km/h'). speed.to_base_units(). Q_(5, 'meter') for Quantity constructor. Dimensionality checking: distance + time raises DimensionalityError. NumPy: 5 * ureg.meter * np.ones(10). Pandas: pint-pandas for unit-aware DataFrames. Custom units. String parsing: ureg('5 km/h'). Serialization. Temperature conversion (non-multiplicative).

gotchas: Temperature unit conversions require .to() not arithmetic (Celsius vs Kelvin offsets)
Custom UnitRegistry instances are not interoperable with default registry
pint-pandas required for DataFrame unit support (separate install)
Some scientific constants may be ambiguous (c the speed of light vs c in custom units)
best_when: Scientific data with mixed units (km/h, m/s, mph conversions)
Catching unit bugs at computation time (DimensionalityError)
Building APIs that accept values with physical units
avoid_when: Pure numeric computation where units are implicit (NumPy faster without Pint overhead)
Simple one-off unit conversions (no library needed)
Performance-critical loops (unit object overhead per operation)
summary: Pint adds physical units to Python numbers and NumPy arrays. ureg = UnitRegistry(); distance = 5 * ureg.meter; speed = distance / (2 * ureg.second); speed.to('km/h'). DimensionalityError when mixing incompatible units. NumPy array support. pint-pandas for unit-aware DataFrames. Temperature non-multiplicative conversions. String parsing: ureg('5 km/h'). Essential for physics/engineering code requiring unit safety. Catches unit bugs at runtime.
